speaker: interviewer
text: The date is September 28, 1995. We're interviewing Helen Rosenblatt, birth name, Diament. Interviewer is Martha Frazer. We're in Riverdale, New York in the United States. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about the liquidation of the small ghetto. You were going to the marketplace.

speaker: interviewee
text: The scene in the marketplace was terrible. Children were crying. I saw children just knocking at the walls and killed. There was a little place where, before the war, was a butcher store. In there, they started already the small killing. I was separated from my husband. They again separated men from the women. and we starting a new march. We heard later on-- being already in the concentration camp, in the labor camp, Hasag-Peltzery, we heard that after we left, they put dynamite underneath of the ghetto, sprung it, and nothing was left. A lot of young people who where the beautiful youth of the Jewish, of Jews, they found there, dead there. Many of them went through the tunnel and survived in the forest continuing their work against the oppressor. Those who were in the marketplace were led to the big factory, munition factory. The name Hasag-Peltzery. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was also a barrack where we were. This was our living quarter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The first moment when we came to a big, big hole, some people went wild. I saw women running unconsciously around screaming. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It-- it was terrible. I was numb, couldn't think, sitting on the floor, waiting for the next. I had to be assigned for work. My-- my group was the [NON-ENGLISH]. It means they remodeled the-- the shells that they brought from the battlefield, adjusting it to new needs. And we had to control them and to work here from early morning. And again, early in the morning, snow or sun or shine, we're standing on the-- on the countdown. And everybody had a number. I forgot my number. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given a number when you went into the factory? Or, had you gotten it while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only there in the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? On ?] the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had quickly to give my number. [INTERPOSING VOICES] I had no name anymore. And I had to go to where they-- I was assigned. There was a long table a lot of women worked around. Our supervisor was a young woman. She was a Volksdeutsch. She-- she favored the German, because she probably was born German. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and she watched us. The other women who worked with me were very simple persons. And they found a way to please her. They gave her complements. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she liked it. And they spoke German. I, in my pride, could not dare to speak a word in German, only Polish. She knew who I am. She knew about my higher education. She knew that I know German. And she really found a way to be my nemesis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mrs. Koch, Koch. She used to come and give me the hardest work that was-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] And-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Now, she found out all to make my life very, very hard. She assigned me to a machine. And it was sabotage to stop the machine. The machine worked from morning to evening. [BACKGROUND NOISE] [SIGHS] [DRINKS] It was very hot. One summer day, it was so hot, I wanted a drink of water. The fountain was outside the building. And I asked the other girl who worked at the other machine to work on both machines. Unfortunately, she came in when I left. And she took me for punishment. We had to go to the-- to a place where-- where a guy, a Ukrainian, was sitting there waiting with his whip to give as many as ordered. I brought in there. I saw only a guy in the corner sitting and drinking some vodka and a big table. He said, lie down. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This ring, I saved. I didn't give it to the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Because this is only memento from my mother. I got it at my high school graduation. So aware of the fact, I put the ring underneath of my tongue and wait-- waited. She said, 12 and left.

speaker: interviewee
text: On her way there, she said, I give you the last chance, speak German, and I forgive you this time. I don't know. I was stupid. I didn't. And she said, 12, and left.

speaker: interviewer
text: The guy was drunk. In his stupor, he mixed me up with my supervisor. And he said, what? You the German girl has to suffer from the Jewish woman? No. I give you a few.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gives me one, a good one. And he said, no more. But when you go back, tell her that you got 12. He gave me even clothes, because I was barefoot. And my dress was falling apart from the lice that lived on my body. I came back to my place and played the role of a-- of a broken and miserable person. And then it was forgotten and went on with work. Now, my brother was assigned to a different group. And this is another story that I want to tell you that miracles happened, that I am not a hero. I am a simple, loving sister. But how I saved his life, this is the story. In this group, one guy ran off at night. In the morning, when they counted people, one is missing. And the punishment came right away. They counted to ten, every tenth, out. Unfortunately, my brother was the number 10. And together with other young guys, they tied his hands in the back, put them outside the barbed wire, in a pit, and waited for the order to be killed. I was at work. And I had no idea what's going on there. And one of the Jewish supervisor said to me quietly, something happened to your brother. Instinctly, I said, I don't care. I have to go and to do-- and to see my brother. This was 11:00 or 12:00 during the day. You could not see no person outside the buildings. And this was a day of inspection. Dr. Lied, the head of the-- of-- of the whole complex, came for inspection. And he's walking. And here is a woman walking and two SS men on both sides with drawn guns. They want to kill me right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, stop it. I want to know why is she here? To him, I spoke German. And I told him this story. My brother is innocent. He worked like all other men. Why? Why do I have to lose him? And then he ironically said, you all Jews are innocent. Why didn't your brother report it that the guy is going to run off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I said, the one who did it made the secret. He saved his life. No one knew about it. My brother could not do it. He was somehow moved by what I said. And he said, don't shoot today. Go back to your work. I'm going to check at the place where all those guys are. Go back. I went back. And it was 11 o'clock. At 12 o'clock, there's a stop of work. And we have the so-called lunch, the-- the dirty potatoes in water. And we always ask, give me from the bottom, not only the water. At this time, I used to go to the place where my boyfriend was. He was there already my husband, because I passed it over that I got married in the ghetto. And I usually came there to him, so we shared at least one meal. That's all we can do. I came in. I told him what happened, sat down on a little block, and didn't know anything what's going on. After a while, a big commotion. A lot of people were running there to me and saying, they all are saved. Dr. Lied came to them. And he said, I met a very courageous sister. And she said that you are innocent. So go there, and thank her for your life. And then I saw my brother. I got into a shock. I didn't see. I couldn't talk. I couldn't move. I thought it's the end. And then I recovered. A little episode that shows how little of worth was a human life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- can you describe the conditions in the factory, the barracks, the food the sleeping conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Sure. [BACKGROUND NOISE] We lived in barracks, on plywood. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Let's stop. Let's-- can we stop?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in barracks-

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: --on plywood. I didn't have anything just my coat. And in this coat, I was going through typhoid fever, too. And I lived in this coat. Every morning when we went outside to be counted, it was so cold. And I had hunger pains.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided to take a piece of the bread this big and save a little piece for the morning. I put my tired head down overnight. And when I opened up my eyes, the bread was gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the first time that I could cry. I cried for the piece of bread, because we turned into animals. And only a primitive instinct how to survive was-- was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got a piece of bread. We got one little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bag, tiny bag, with sugar. When they gave us this sugar once a week, on our way, we-- we made sure that it's inside. We had a meal during 12 o'clock, potatoes, dirty potatoes. And then at 6:00, we returned to our barracks to start again the next morning. You worked from 6:00 to 6:00, about a 12 hour day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, after 600, I could get in touch or see my brother or to see my husband. That's all that we could only to hope for better. My brother, being a very active member of the underground--

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't worry.

speaker: interviewee
text: --got some help from the outside. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Poles were sent to work in our factory. So through the Poles, we could smuggle in a little bit of food. But I didn't have money to buy the food. The boxes with the shells were covered with a kind of plastic. And this plastic was a good plan how to find money. I ask one Pole to bring me a scissor. He brought me next morning a scissor. I cut the plastic, made little wallets, and sold it to the Poles. I had money. And for the money, they brought me a little bit of food. My brother got much more food. So one day he visited us after 6 o'clock and brought us a whole loaf of bread. Did we have a ball then. We ate. Talking about bread, I had once during the time in the barracks a dream. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the dream, I went through a door into a bakery. And there were loaves of bread. And I ate and ate and ate, not stopping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then another door opened up. And somebody showed me an album with photos of my family, pushing it into my eyes and saying, remember us, remember us. I woke up. And I told my dream to everybody, so it was a kind of a vision. OK. Where should we go on?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remained working in this factory through the rest of the German occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during this whole time your brother continued to be in the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, through contact. But nobody knew about it. And he got some money to buy a little bit of food. But we survived on what we had. There was no place to wash ourselves. We have to go only once a week to get a shower. And the lice had a picnic.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you actually had typhoid there and just recovered on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was earlier. In the very beginning, through the lice, I was-- I became sick. To the infirmary, they brought us in what we had. I-- without any help, people were dying around me. In high fever, without any help, I survived. Another miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Through the-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] [INAUDIBLE] Through the underground, were you able to-- through the underground-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Stop. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is the second slate on take number three. The date is September 28, 1995. We're interviewing Helen Rosenblatt, birth name Diament. The interviewer is Martha Fraser. We're in New York, in Riverdale, New York in the United States. And we're speaking English.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, as the war was coming to an end-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] Sh.

speaker: interviewer
text: As the war was coming to an end, did-- did you get any information in the factory as to what was happening around you, what the progress of the war was?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know anything until the final day of January the 15, 16 in 1945. We saw fires around the factory. We didn't know why. And then we saw the German packing and running. And they screamed to us, children, come with us! But before they made also a selection who is going with them and who will die here. My husband was in a group called [NON-ENGLISH]. Because the men, they wanted to save the slaves that didn't cost a penny, and they can work in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they collected all men, put them in a place. And the train was waiting already, because we were very close to the railroad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was the last time I saw my husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he went with the men. And I was in the next transport for the women in hope that we will meet again someplace near. The fire spread. And we heard a lot of bombardment. And it was early in the morning. And then they didn't send us. They left us in a big pit ready for the transport. But it was for them too late. They took the train going to Germany. And I was hoping that when I walk out behind the wire, there he will be, and we continue again our journey. Unfortunately, it didn't happen this way. The Russian came. The Germans were gone. The Russians opened the gates of the factory, of the place, of the labor camp. They did not do anything for us. They said, go. And here, the end of my suffering and the beginning of a knew tragedy. I was alone. Everybody was gone, my brother, my husband. And I am alone. And I was free, but alone. Now, we had to leave this place. And it was wintertime, deep snow. How we looked, you can imagine. The Poles that lived around this stretch of way closed up there shutters and their gates. Because the white people are coming out. I was carrying a little-- a little bag. Not carrying, but pulling it through the snow. No one came to help us, to give us food, to ask if we are sick, if we cannot work until we came to a hospital, a Russian hospital. And we begged them for food. And this is what they answered. You work, you get food. And when they started giving us food, a lot of people died. Because this was not the food for us. We had to work in the basement to peel potatoes that often we stole the few potatoes to take them. And 25 people who-- who belonged to the underground, we got into one big room, slept on the floor, and waited what will happen next. This was in January. And news from the front came that it's almost over. We knew about it. Until May '45, we knew this is the end of the war. And now, with great hopes, I waited for my husband and for my brother. It took a long time. And to kill my sorrow and to grow according to my profession, I opened up a little school in this room where we lived for those youngsters who survived, who came out from bunkers. And I worked with them until June '45. Then I decided to go and look for my family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- the trains didn't run properly. There was only one train going through the-- through the border of Czechoslovakia, Poland and Czechoslovakia. And everybody had to present a document. We had only from the Jewish counsel a document written in Polish that we are-- that we are going to look for our families. This was the document. And people packed into the trains until the Russian general came and asked for our documents.

speaker: interviewer
text: He couldn't read it. So he went to his office. Meanwhile, the train left. And here I am with my friend, standing on the trucks and asking, what next? A Polish worker came over to us both. And what did we have? In our knapsack, we had two loaves of bread and one carton of cigarettes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, if you walk only a little bit, you pass a bridge, and you be in Czechoslovakia. So start walking. And we walking along the-- the trucks. It's getting dark already. And we're losing ground, because the bridge was not there anymore. And we're following, and we hear from other side, we're going to shoot you. From the Czechoslovakia side, the soldiers who watched the border. And we begged them, please, don't shoot. We tell you everything. We are not German. We are Jews. Please help us out from between those stones. They brought us into a-- a little place where soldiers sitting on the [NON-ENGLISH]. And they looked into our knapsack. And they found cigarettes. And they were dying for a cigarette. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we gave them the cigarettes. And what happened later--
